evals.name: who-can-delete-secrets-prod
evals.prompt: Who can delete secrets in our prod clusters?
evals.expected_output: Runs `cub-rbac preflight`, then `cub-rbac who-can delete secrets` scoped to prod with --target-where (e.g. "Slug LIKE 'prod-%'"). Reports the subjects with their cluster, binding/role, and namespace scope. Does not mutate; does not use kubectl auth can-i.

evals.name: who-can-exec-pods
evals.prompt: Which subjects can exec into pods?
evals.expected_output: Runs `cub-rbac who-can create pods/exec` (recognizing exec is the pods/exec subresource with the create verb), reports the matching subjects with provenance. Does not mutate.

evals.name: subject-access-serviceaccount
evals.prompt: What can the ci-deployer service account in the apps namespace do?
evals.expected_output: Runs `cub-rbac access ServiceAccount:apps/ci-deployer` and reports every role the SA holds across the fleet with cluster and scope. Uses the ServiceAccount:namespace/name subject format. Does not mutate.

evals.name: named-group-resource
evals.expected_output: Runs `cub-rbac who-can update deployments --api-group apps`, correctly supplying the apps API group rather than querying the core group. Reports the subjects.

evals.name: does-not-invent-builtin-access
evals.prompt: Does the binding to the view ClusterRole let anyone read secrets?
evals.expected_output: Explains that cub-rbac only credits cluster-admin among Kubernetes builtins, so a who-can query will not show secrets access via the view role, and that bindings to view/admin/edit are surfaced by rbac-findings instead. Does not fabricate a grant.
